name: Stacy Rogers
occupation: Business owner (Kids Closet / KCC North America, Eberly Farm)
religion: Christian (non-denominational; identifies as a "Child of God" on social media)
education: Newton High School; 15 years of pharmacy and medical field experience
family: Mother of three adult sons, two of whom have learning challenges. Lt. Governor running mate: Michael Smith.
whoTheyAre:
Stacy Rogers is a Wichita business owner and the first Republican to declare her candidacy for the 2026 Kansas governor's race. Born in Wichita and raised in Newton, she graduated from Newton High School and spent 15 years working in pharmacy and medical settings before turning to business ownership. She is a mother of three sons, two of whom have learning challenges, and that experience shaped much of how she talks about schools and family policy. She homeschooled her children with special needs and also used public and private schools, giving her a firsthand look at all three systems.

Rogers owns Eberly Farm, a Wichita-area event venue, and runs Kid's Closet Wichita and its parent company KCC North America, a nationwide consignment sales franchise that hosts large events for gently used children's clothing, toys, and furniture. Running a national franchise system is a real organizational achievement, and Rogers leans on that business background as the core of her pitch to voters. She also served as Water Commissioner for Mount Hope, Kansas from 2007 to 2012, sat on the Sedgwick County Developmental Disability Organization board, and worked as a parent match with Families Together, helping families advocate for children with learning challenges.

Rogers declared her candidacy on November 6, 2024, and was the first candidate to officially file paperwork with the Kansas Secretary of State in December 2025. Her running mate, Michael Smith, made them the first governor-lieutenant governor team to file. She presents herself as a political outsider who wants to bring a business owner's mindset to state government, with a focus on straight talk and low-drama politics.
theirRecord:
Rogers does not have a legislative voting record. Her public service history is limited to her time as Water Commissioner in Mount Hope (2007-2012) and her board work with the Sedgwick County Developmental Disability Organization. Because she has never served in the Kansas Legislature or held statewide office, there is no trail of floor votes, committee decisions, or bill sponsorships to review. This is a genuine gap compared to the career officeholders in the Republican primary field.

Her campaign record is built almost entirely on her business experience and her early entry into the race. She was the first Republican to declare and the first to file, which her campaign presents as evidence of initiative and commitment. Her platform centers on issuing a Day One executive order to audit every state department for waste and fraud, addressing rising property taxes, sunsetting STAR bonds, returning cigarette and alcohol tax revenue to Kansas counties, supporting local agriculture, and protecting life with expanded resources for parents and affordable healthcare.

The honest assessment is that Rogers is running on what she would do, not on what she has done in public office. That is not necessarily disqualifying, but it means voters have to evaluate her on her business track record, her personal story, and her campaign promises rather than on a proven government record.
whatYouShouldKnow: Rogers was the first Republican to declare for the 2026 governor's race (November 6, 2024) and the first candidate of either party to officially file paperwork with the Kansas Secretary of State (December 2025).

She owns and operates KCC North America (Kid's Closet), a nationwide consignment sales franchise with events in cities across the country, and Eberly Farm, a Wichita-area event venue. Running a national franchise is a more substantial business credential than it might first appear.

Two of her three sons have learning challenges, and she homeschooled them. This personal experience drives her education and family policy positions and her work advocating for families of children with disabilities.

Her campaign finance filing shows roughly $24,863 in total receipts and $14,470 cash on hand, which is far below the top-tier governor candidates who have raised $900,000 to $2.5 million. She loaned her own campaign $2,398 and has no major donor base on the public record.

She served as Water Commissioner for Mount Hope, Kansas (2007-2012) and sat on the Sedgwick County Developmental Disability Organization board, but has never held a state legislative or statewide office.

Her campaign platform includes a Day One executive order to audit every state department for waste and fraud, sunsetting STAR bonds, returning alcohol and cigarette tax revenue (ALBTR) to Kansas counties, property tax relief, and a pro-life stance with expanded family resources.

No specific church affiliation or denomination has been publicly verified. She identifies as a "Child of God" on social media, and faith language appears in her public profile, but no congregation is named in any available source.
If elected, she would be the first female Republican governor in Kansas history.
whereTheyWorship: No public church affiliation has been verified for Stacy Rogers in any of the sources reviewed. She identifies as a "Child of God" on her social media profiles, and faith language is present in her public communications, but she has not publicly named a specific congregation, denomination, or house of worship. Her campaign materials do not reference a church home. This is a notable gap in a Kansas Republican primary where faith credentials matter to many voters.

quotes.text: I cannot stand silent and watch small businesses and homeowners be destroyed through high property taxes.
Go through each Department with a fine tooth comb, root out inefficiencies, mishandling and fraud.
Families should have the right to make the medical decisions that benefit themselves and their kids, even if those decisions are different from what government bureaucrats recommend.
Straight Talk. Real Issues. For All Kansans.

campaignFinance.narrative: Rogers is running one of the smallest campaigns in the governor's race by the numbers. Her Kansas Ethics Commission filing shows $24,863 in total receipts, $11,073 in expenditures, and $14,470 cash on hand. Of her receipts, $20,989 came from itemized contributions and $3,873 from unitemized small donations. She also received $1,200 in in-kind contributions for video production services. Rogers loaned her own campaign $2,398. This is a shoestring operation compared to frontrunners like Jeff Colyer ($2.07 million raised), Philip Sarnecki ($2.53 million), or even Joy Eakins ($1.14 million). There is no sign of large-donor concentration or major PAC support.

donors.name: Shae's Point of View (in-kind video services)

campaignFinance.undisclosed: $3,873 in unitemized contributions (donors giving under the itemization threshold).
